subject: AP English Literature and Composition
source_note: Structured from the AP English Literature and Composition Course and Exam Description (Effective Fall 2024). Essential questions and skill summaries below are paraphrased in original wording, not copied from the CED. Example texts are drawn from the CED's optional sample-activity suggestions; they are not a required reading list.

units.title: Unit 1: Short Fiction I
essential_questions: How do specific textual details reveal a character's perspective and motives?
How does a story's setting convey more than just time and place?
How does the order of events in a plot shape a reader's understanding?
Who is telling this story, and how does that choice affect what the reader knows?
focus_skills: Identify what textual details reveal about a character, their perspective, and their motives
Identify what textual details reveal about a setting
Explain how plot orders events and why that sequence matters
Identify the narrator or speaker of a text and distinguish narrator from author
Write a paragraph with a defensible claim and supporting textual evidence
texts: Kate Chopin, "The Story of an Hour"

units.title: Unit 2: Poetry I
essential_questions: How do line and stanza breaks shape the way a poem delivers meaning?
How do similes and metaphors ask a reader to transfer meaning from one thing to another?
How does a shift or contrast within a poem signal a turn in meaning?
focus_skills: Explain how a poem's structure (lines, stanzas) shapes its meaning
Explain the function of a contrast or shift within a text
Identify and explain the function of a simile
Identify and explain the function of a metaphor
Explain the function of specific word choices, including repetition and ambiguous references
texts: Jimmy Santiago Baca, "I Am Offering This Poem"

units.title: Unit 3: Longer Fiction or Drama I
essential_questions: How does an extended narrative develop character and conflict differently than a short story does?
What makes a thesis statement defensible, and how does a line of reasoning connect evidence to it?
How do internal and external conflicts reveal a character's values?
focus_skills: Explain the function of a character changing or remaining unchanged
Explain the function of conflict (internal and external) in a text
Develop a thesis statement that conveys a defensible interpretation
Develop commentary that connects evidence to a line of reasoning
Select relevant, sufficient evidence to support an argument
texts: Zora Neale Hurston, Their Eyes Were Watching God

units.title: Unit 4: Short Fiction II
essential_questions: How do contrasting characters, like a protagonist and antagonist or foils, reveal competing values?
How does narrative distance or stream-of-consciousness narration shape what a reader experiences?
How do specific word choices reveal a narrator's or speaker's tone?
focus_skills: Explain the function of contrasting characters
Describe how textual details reveal complexity in a character's relationships
Identify and describe narrative distance and stream-of-consciousness narration
Identify details, diction, or syntax that reveal a narrator's or speaker's tone
Develop a thesis statement and a supporting line of reasoning
texts: Kate Chopin, "The Story of an Hour"

units.title: Unit 5: Poetry II
essential_questions: How does an extended metaphor build meaning across an entire poem rather than a single line?
How do patterns of imagery work together to convey a larger idea?
Why might two readers reach different, equally defensible interpretations of the same poem?
focus_skills: Distinguish literal from figurative meaning
Identify and explain imagery, extended metaphor, personification, and allusion
Explain how open versus closed poetic structures shape meaning
Revise a thesis and line of reasoning in response to an alternative interpretation
texts: Elizabeth Barrett Browning, "How Do I Love Thee?"

units.title: Unit 6: Longer Fiction or Drama II
essential_questions: How does a nonlinear structure (flashback, foreshadowing, in medias res) change a reader's experience of a story?
What makes a narrator reliable or unreliable, and how does that affect interpretation?
How does an object or character come to function as a symbol?
focus_skills: Explain the function of foil characters and inconsistencies within a character
Identify structures that interrupt chronology and explain their effect on the reader
Explain how a narrator's reliability affects interpretation of a narrative
Identify and explain the function of a symbol
Write commentary that connects evidence to a line of reasoning in a complex argument
texts: Zora Neale Hurston, Their Eyes Were Watching God

units.title: Unit 7: Short Fiction III
essential_questions: How does a text's treatment of social structures or institutions shape its meaning?
How does pacing, what's revealed and when, shape a reader's emotional experience?
When should a reader revise their interpretation instead of just noting an alternative one?
focus_skills: Explain the function of a character changing or remaining unchanged, including sudden change (epiphany)
Explain how setting and pacing contribute to meaning in a narrative
Identify and explain symbols, motifs, and figurative language in prose
Revise a thesis and line of reasoning when evidence doesn't fully support it

units.title: Unit 8: Poetry III
essential_questions: How does a conceit build an extended, often surprising comparison across a poem?
How do irony and paradox create tension between what's said and what's meant?
How can ambiguity in language support more than one valid interpretation?
focus_skills: Explain how structure and punctuation shape meaning at both the part and whole-poem level
Identify and explain juxtaposition, irony, and paradox
Explain how ambiguous language invites multiple interpretations
Write a literary argument that accounts for a text's complexity
texts: Metaphysical poetry (e.g. John Donne) featuring extended conceits

units.title: Unit 9: Longer Fiction or Drama III
essential_questions: How does a character's response to a story's resolution reveal their underlying values?
How do multiple, even conflicting, narrator perspectives contribute to a text's complexity?
What separates a competent literary argument from a genuinely sophisticated one?
focus_skills: Explain how a character's choices at the story's resolution reveal complexity
Explain the function of a significant event or conflict's resolution, or lack of resolution
Analyze shifts in a narrator's or speaker's perspective over the course of a long text
Develop a sophisticated literary argument that situates an interpretation in a broader context
texts: Zora Neale Hurston, Their Eyes Were Watching God
